sanskrit_devanagari:
सर्वद्वाराणि संयम्य मनो हृदि निरुध्य च ।
मूर्ध्न्याधायात्मनः प्राणमास्थितो योगधारणाम् ॥ १२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
sarva-dvārāṇi saṃyamya mano hṛdi nirudhya ca |
mūrdhny ādhāyātmanaḥ prāṇam āsthito yoga-dhāraṇām || 12 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase renders verse as concatenated text but body matches the canonical reading word-for-word).
exact (Mukundananda house style: h-aspirate marks on śh, single danda visarga; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_08.012 = MBh_06,030.012). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: all the gates / all the doors of the body (the senses)

translations.verbatim_quote: The yogic situation is that of detachment from all sensual engagements. Closing all the doors of the senses and fixing the mind on the heart and the life air at the top of the head, one establishes himself in yoga.
BG 8.12: Restraining all the gates of the body and fixing the mind in the heart region, and then drawing the life-breath to the head, one should get established in steadfast yogic concentration.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: To practice yoga as suggested here, one first has to close the doors of all sense enjoyment. This practice is called pratyāhāra, or withdrawing the senses from the sense objects. The sense organs for acquiring knowledge – the eyes, ears, nose, tongue and touch – should be fully controlled and should not be allowed to e

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use, ≤300 chars of full bhāṣya per IP rules)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.12 Samyamya, having controlled; sarva-dvarani, all the passages, the doors of perception; niruddhya, having confined; the manah, mind; hrdi, in the heart — not allowing it to spread out; and after that, with the help of the mind controlled therein, rising up through the nerve running upward.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.12 Subduing all the senses like ear etc., which constitute the ‘doorways’ for sense impressions, i.e., withdrawing them from their natural functions; holding the mind in Me, the imperishable ‘seated within the lotus of the heart’; practising steady abstraction of mind (Dhāraṇā).

positions.rendering: yogic concentration: the mind, having been confined in the heart, is then directed upward through the nāḍī (the suṣumnā) into the head — this is the steady fixation of yoga the verse names
steady abstraction of mind (dhāraṇā), the practice of fixing the mind on the imperishable seated within the lotus of the heart
concentration as the technique that makes the prāṇa-control practical: the verse describes the necessary preparation, not the final act

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Traditions agree on the technical content of yoga-dhāraṇām (a steady, sustained fixation). They differ on what exactly is fixed-upon: the supreme self (Shankara), the imperishable in the heart-lotus (Ramanuja), or the immediate technique of breath-discipline (Sridhara). All read 8.12 as preparation for 8.13.

literal_meaning: Having controlled all the gates (of the senses), having confined the mind in the heart, having drawn the life-breath up into the head, established in yogic concentration —
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse begins a single sentence (8.12-8.13) describing the meditative practice at the time of departure. The senses are withdrawn from their objects (sarva-dvārāṇi saṃyamya); the mind is held in the heart (mano hṛdi nirudhya); the prāṇa is drawn up to the head (mūrdhni ādhāya); the practitioner stands fixed in yogic concentration (āsthito yoga-dhāraṇām). Shankara, Ramanuja, and the other commentators read this as preparatory: the verse is one half of the instruction, the second half (8.13) names the syllable to be uttered. The traditions agree on the technical structure of the contemplative practice; differences in commentary concern who can perform it and what is being meditated upon.
